evidence_gaps: No analytics, site search logs, support tickets, or prior research were provided.
Analysis is based on visible IA and page content only; real user frequency is inferred, not observed.
The page is a campaign-style landing page with many outbound videos/press releases, so end-to-end task completion is limited on-page.
No direct evidence was available on which audience segments arrive most often or from which channels.

task_longlist.task_statement: See what MAHA changed this year

evidence.element: Page title, hero, and by-the-numbers section
evidence.note: The page is explicitly framed as a year-in-review summary and highlights major outcomes immediately.

task_longlist.rationale: This is the dominant page purpose and the clearest public-facing job-to-be-done. The site strongly supports scanning and understanding summary claims, but not always completing deeper verification without leaving the page.

evidence.element: Making Our Children Healthy Again card
evidence.note: A prominently placed section highlights a strategy with more than 120 initiatives on childhood chronic disease.

task_longlist.rationale: Children’s health is a central theme on the page and likely a high-interest topic for parents, caregivers, and general readers. The topic is easy to find but deeper completion depends on outbound content.

task_longlist.task_statement: See if drug prices may drop

evidence.note: The card promises significantly reduced prescription costs and links to a press release.
The linked release explains the pricing action and gives users a deeper evidence path.

task_longlist.rationale: Prescription affordability is a broad public concern with high stakes. The task is fairly easy to locate and can be pursued further through the linked release.

evidence.element: Real food, food dyes, dairy dyes, and GRAS sections
evidence.note: The page clusters multiple food-supply and ingredient reform topics, signaling strong user relevance.
The linked release explains the GRAS rulemaking direction and transparency rationale.

task_longlist.rationale: Food ingredient transparency appears repeatedly and is likely important to families and health-conscious users. The site supports both overview and deeper reading via linked releases.

evidence.note: The page calls out the report as a flagship artifact and provides a press-release path.

task_longlist.rationale: Evidence-seeking users, journalists, advocates, and policy followers will want the report itself. The report is clearly signposted, though the landing page does not surface all document details inline.

evidence.element: Updating Childhood Vaccine Schedule and related vaccine sections
evidence.note: The page includes several vaccine-related cards, indicating likely public demand for policy-change information.

task_longlist.rationale: The public stakes are high because vaccination guidance affects family health decisions. The topic is visible, but the page itself gives only brief summaries before users must navigate out.

task_longlist.task_statement: See what changed for infant formula

evidence.note: The card addresses safety and supply options for infant formula, a focused but important family need.

task_longlist.rationale: Important for families with infants, but narrower than the top cross-cutting themes. The page supports awareness more than full completion.

evidence.note: The card highlights reduced red tape and improved care, which are meaningful to patients navigating coverage.

task_longlist.rationale: The topic matters to many insured users, but it is one of many equal-weight cards rather than a dominant page path.

task_longlist.task_statement: Find updates on rural health funding

evidence.note: The page explicitly highlights a $50 billion rural health transformation program.

task_longlist.rationale: Highly relevant to a smaller segment of users. The information is visible and clear but not developed into an end-to-end service path.

evidence.element: Repeated Watch the video links across cards
evidence.note: Video is the dominant interaction pattern after scanning summaries.

task_longlist.rationale: The site strongly supports this behavior, but it is usually a means of consuming campaign content rather than a high-consequence user need.

evidence.element: Global search access and FAQ link in footer
evidence.note: The page exposes search and an Answers hub for users whose needs extend beyond the campaign content.
The linked Answers page offers FAQ-style paths for common public questions.

task_longlist.rationale: This is a fallback task when the landing page does not meet a user’s need. It is supported, but not the page’s main job.

task_longlist.task_statement: Explore broader HHS programs and services

evidence.note: Programs & Services, Laws & Regulations, and other sitewide nav options are visible from the page.

task_longlist.rationale: Useful when user intent broadens from MAHA to HHS services generally, but still a secondary site-navigation task.

evidence.note: The page includes a clear subscribe CTA.
The linked form asks for first name, email, and update preferences.

task_longlist.rationale: The form is functional, but subscribing is peripheral to the core information-seeking purpose of this page.

evidence.note: The footer offers multiple social follow links.

task_longlist.rationale: Social following is easy to do but low-consequence and not central to the page’s public-service value.

evidence.element: Contact HHS footer link and call center information
evidence.note: The page exposes generic contact options, but not MAHA-specific support flows.

task_longlist.rationale: Generic contact is available, but it is buried in the footer and not tailored to the main content themes.

recommended_survey.instructions: Show respondents the full task list in random order. Ask them to first choose the five tasks that matter most when visiting this page or section, then choose the five that matter least. Use plain-language task labels only, with no organizational jargon, and rotate order to reduce primacy bias.
task_list_for_voting: See what MAHA changed this year
See if drug prices may drop
See what changed for infant formula
Find updates on rural health funding
Explore broader HHS programs and services
target_segments: People interested in food, nutrition, and ingredient safety
Rural residents and rural health stakeholders
General public visitors arriving from news or social links

next_steps: Validate the draft task list with a Top Tasks survey using forced-choice top-five and bottom-five selection.
Compare survey winners against analytics, search logs, and referral data to confirm real demand.
Turn the top 3-5 tasks into primary navigation or landing-page modules with stronger evidence paths.
Reduce or group low-value campaign and social-follow elements so they do not compete with top tasks.
For top tasks, add clearer end-to-end completion paths such as report downloads, FAQs, and action-specific guidance.
summary: The clearest top tasks are to understand what MAHA changed this year, find actions on children’s health, see whether drug prices may drop, check changes to food ingredients, read the MAHA report, and review vaccine schedule changes. The page strongly supports scanning campaign updates, but many deeper tasks require leaving the page for videos or press releases. Key risks are weak end-to-end completion, no visible prioritization among many equal cards, and limited evidence of real user demand. Next, validate the longlist with a Top Tasks survey and compare winners with analytics and search behavior.
